results.title: Fabraix: The world's frontier hacker for AI agents.
results.content:
Our first challenge, The Gatekeeper, is live now. Kai is an assistant guarding a classified access code. You can start playing without an account, but you’ll need to sign in for a successful break to count toward the weekly leaderboard. We review every submission ourselves. The player with the most approved breaks each week wins, and we publish a new challenge every Monday.

Playground is open source, including the client, a reference implementation of the defender engine, and every challenge configuration. You can also propose a future challenge.

We made Playground public because other people will try attacks our team would never think of. We’ll use what we learn from successful breaks to improve how AI agents are tested and defended. [...] Fabraix

Launched on May 8th, 2026

23

223

Fastlane
Gaurav Thapa

#### What's great

We had to run testing engagements for enterprise customers which would have set us back 10's of thousands of dollars and would have taken weeks to conclude. We tried Fabraix instead and their fully autonomous solution started showing value in hours and concluded in days with a fraction of the cost.

#### What needs improvement

Would be great if the support tier for startups had an SLA instead of just the discord support

#### vs Alternatives

⁠We have considered other bespoke consulting solutions which would have been tailored to what our multi-modal agent since no other automated solutions exists for our agents.

ElevenAgents by ElevenLabs

What do you think? …

Fabraix

Hey Product Hunt 👋 Zach here, co-founder of Fabraix.

We build frontier red-teaming AI agents that find security vulnerabilities in customer-facing AI. Playground turns part of that work into a game anyone can play.

Each challenge is a live AI agent with real tools, including web search and browser access. It has a secret it has been instructed to protect, and we publish its full system prompt. You can see exactly what the agent was told and try to get around its defenses.

results.title: Frontier Risk Report (February to March 2026)
results.content:
#### Reward Hacking

Each program has a small held-out set of hidden test cases that are highly similar to the visible test cases. Agents are deemed to have succeeded if they (a) pass above a threshold (95%) of visible test cases, while (b) the visible test case score is not >5% higher (in absolute terms) than the hidden test case score. The latter allows us to detect when agents implement non-general solutions to these tasks, for example using a lookup table of inputs to outputs. [...] a single problem. The agent reported its reward hacking approach when submitting its solution. [...] Training incentives. These behaviors can be traced to agents’ training incentives. Reinforcement learning (RL) with automated graders can incentivize “reward hacking” to exploit flaws in the graders,30 while RL on human or AI feedback can reward sycophancy, manipulation, and distorting evidence of performance.31 In responses to our questionnaire, companies reported seeing failure modes like “circumvent[ing] constraints,” “falsifying verification,” “deliberate deception aimed at hiding underperformance or failure to complete a task,” “lying to users about task completion,” and “reckless excessive measures[…] to complete a difficult user-specified task”, which we infer to be a result of these training incentives.
